company_persona: a consumer fintech (debit card + P2P payments) whose LLM chatbot handles most support volume
title: Refunds from thin air
prompt: You're the PM for Finchly Assist, the AI support chatbot at Finchly, a consumer fintech offering debit cards and peer-to-peer payments. The bot resolves about 60% of our 45,000 weekly support chats without a human. Over the past two weeks, compliance has flagged a serious problem: the bot is confidently telling customers about refund policies that don't exist — one user was promised a "$50 goodwill credit" we've never offered, another a 24-hour chargeback reversal. Screenshots are circulating on social media, and legal says written refund promises may be legally binding, so we're honoring them. Leadership wants a plan by end of day. Walk me through how you'd approach it.

hidden_facts.incident_scope: 62 confirmed fabrication incidents so far, ALL in refund/dispute/chargeback intents (about 18% of chat volume, ~8,100 of the 45,000 weekly chats). Balance inquiries, card activation, login help — zero confirmed incidents.
hidden_facts.onset_timing: First confirmed incident was 12 days ago; steady daily trickle since. Thirteen days ago the help-center content team ran a big article migration (renamed and restructured all dispute/refund policy pages).
hidden_facts.retrieval_health: If asked about the RAG layer: for refund/dispute queries, the top retrieved passage now scores below the relevance threshold in ~70% of cases — the migrated dispute articles were never re-synced into the vector index. Retrieval for every other category is healthy (>95% above threshold).
hidden_facts.grounding_behavior: The system prompt says 'be helpful and resolve the customer's issue.' There is no instruction to only state policies found in retrieved documents, no citation requirement, and no abstain-and-escalate rule when retrieval comes back empty — the model fills the gap from memory, fluently and confidently.
hidden_facts.model_swap_red_herring: Ten days ago, 30% of traffic was moved to a cheaper distilled model to cut cost per resolved chat from $0.19 to $0.11. Incidents split roughly 30/70 across the two models — proportional to traffic — and the first incidents predate the swap by two days. The model change is not the driver.
hidden_facts.eval_state: The offline eval suite is 400 golden Q&A pairs scored for helpfulness and tone. Only 12 touch refund policy, and there are zero test cases where the correct answer is 'that policy does not exist' or 'I need to escalate.' Groundedness/faithfulness is not measured offline or online.
hidden_facts.csat_trap: CSAT and thumbs-up on refund/dispute chats actually ROSE two points in this window — customers love being promised money. Feedback metrics will not catch this failure mode.
hidden_facts.containment_options: An existing intent classifier detects refund/dispute intents with 92% precision / 89% recall and can route at the start of a chat. Human handling costs ~$4.50 per contact vs ~$0.15 per bot resolution. Capacity, stated precisely: humans currently handle ~18,000 of the 45,000 weekly chats, and staffing can absorb roughly +8% of that HUMAN-HANDLED volume — about 1,400 extra contacts per week. Routing all ~8,100 weekly refund/dispute chats to humans would add ~4,900 net-new human contacts (the ~60% the bot currently resolves), roughly 3.5x the available headroom — full human routing is not feasible at current staffing. Engineering can ship a locked-template flow for refund/dispute intents within a day: verified policy snippets only, no free-form generation, with an escalate-to-human option for anything the templates don't cover.
hidden_facts.latency_cost_budget: Bot latency is p50 2.1s / p95 6.0s. A second-pass groundedness checker on money-related answers would add ~1.2s at p95 and ~$0.03 per conversation. Financial exposure from honored fake promises is ~$30k so far and growing ~$2-3k per day — and legal warns the honored-credit dollars are not the real risk: a visible pattern of binding written promises invites regulator scrutiny, dispute-process complaints, and brand damage with an effectively unbounded tail.
hidden_facts.external_noise: No competitor launch, no fraud spike, no unusual dispute volume — inbound intent mix is flat. The social-media screenshots amplified awareness but the underlying incident rate has been stable since onset.

ideal_answer_notes: Core (expected within 20 minutes): Scope it like an incident, not a metrics exercise — ask how many cases, which intents, and since when. Discover it is concentrated entirely in refund/dispute intents (~18% of volume, ~8,100 chats/week) starting ~12 days ago, and name the legal/compliance exposure as the reason speed matters more than a perfect diagnosis.

Core: Contain before diagnosing, and do the capacity math out loud: routing all ~8,100 weekly refund/dispute chats to humans means ~4,900 net-new contacts against only ~1,400/week of headroom (+8% of the 18,000 human-handled chats) — full human routing is infeasible. Propose a hybrid shipped today via the 92%-precision classifier: a locked-template flow serving only verified policy text, escalating the remainder to humans within headroom. Defend it on the unbounded legal/brand tail of binding written promises, not per-contact cost — full routing would run ~$36k/week, more than the entire $30k exposure to date, while the hybrid costs a few thousand a week.

Core: Debug the pipeline in order — retrieval, then prompting/grounding, then model. Asking about knowledge-base or retrieval changes surfaces the core signal: the help-center migration 13 days ago broke indexing for dispute articles, so ~70% of refund queries retrieve nothing relevant and the model answers from parametric memory.

Core: Explicitly test and dismiss the red herring: the distilled-model rollout is tempting, but incidents split proportionally to its 30% traffic share and the first incidents predate the swap by two days. Say out loud why that evidence exonerates the model change.

Core: Flag that CSAT and thumbs-up are actively misleading here (users like fake refund promises), and define real metrics instead: groundedness/faithfulness rate against retrieved sources, correct-abstention rate, escalation rate, and retrieval hit-rate by intent.

Core: Fix the root cause with minimum guardrails and a numeric relaunch bar: re-sync the vector index and add a CI check tying KB migrations to re-indexing; require money-related answers to be grounded in a retrieved passage (with citation); abstain-and-escalate when retrieval confidence is below threshold; and before re-enabling the bot on refund intents, expand the eval beyond 12 refund questions with adversarial 'this policy does not exist' cases and a bar such as zero fabricated monetary commitments in N=500 adversarial runs with >95% correct abstention.

Stretch (strong-signal differentiator — do not penalize omission in 20 minutes): Build a quick error taxonomy from sampled transcripts — fabricated policy that never existed vs. wrong number on a real policy vs. stale policy — pinning a working definition of 'hallucination' so the fix and the metric target the actual failure class (here: confident fabrication with no source).

Stretch: Reason through the optional second-pass groundedness checker applied to refund intents only — +1.2s p95 and +$0.03 per conversation is trivially worth it on this money-related slice, and scoping it to one intent protects latency and cost everywhere else.

Stretch: Stage the re-rollout — shadow mode with humans reviewing bot drafts on refund intents, then 5% live with automated groundedness monitoring and alerts on retrieval-miss rate, then a ramp gated on those metrics — and close with the postmortem process change so the next KB migration can't silently blind the bot.
extra_checklist: Candidate does the containment feasibility math — recognizes that routing all refund/dispute chats to humans (~4,900 net-new contacts/week) exceeds the ~1,400/week headroom — and proposes a hybrid (locked-template flow plus partial human escalation), defending it on the legal/brand tail risk of binding promises rather than a per-contact cost comparison against the $30k exposure.

Candidate probes the retrieval layer — asks about knowledge-base changes or retrieval hit-rates — and connects the help-center migration to the fabrications, rather than stopping at 'the model hallucinates' or blaming the distilled-model swap.
Candidate specifies a measurable groundedness or abstention eval with a numeric re-launch bar and a staged rollout gate, and names why CSAT/thumbs cannot serve as the success metric.

anchors.strong: Contains first and does the math: spots that full human routing (~4,900 net-new contacts/week) blows past the ~1,400 headroom, so proposes a locked-template flow with human escalation, justified by the unbounded legal tail of binding promises. Walks the pipeline retrieval-first, finds the help-center migration that broke indexing, dismisses the distilled-model red herring via proportional split and timing, replaces misleading CSAT with groundedness and abstention metrics, and gates relaunch on an adversarial eval with a numeric bar.
anchors.at_bar: Reaches containment and the retrieval root cause with nudges — initially fixates on the model swap until Ken shares the proportional split, then asks about knowledge-base changes. Proposes human routing for refund intents but needs prompting to notice the capacity shortfall before landing on a restricted flow. Adds refund test cases yet omits abstention tests and a numeric launch gate; recognizes the compliance stakes, though monitoring stays vague.
anchors.weak: Jumps straight to 'swap the model back' or 'add don't-hallucinate to the prompt' without scoping incidents by intent or timeline. Chases the distilled-model red herring, never asks about retrieval or the help-center migration, and cites rising CSAT as proof the problem is overblown. Offers either no containment despite legally binding promises or routes everything to humans without checking capacity, then re-ships to full traffic with no groundedness eval or launch gate.

teaser: Your fintech's support bot is confidently promising customers refunds that don't exist.
